examples.title: Creating Tables with Integrity Constraints
examples.description: SQL script demonstrating all major integrity constraints in a Student-Course-Enrollment database
examples.code:
-- Department Table (Parent)
CREATE TABLE Department (
    DeptID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    DeptName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    Location VARCHAR(30) DEFAULT 'Delhi'
);

-- Student Table
CREATE TABLE Student (
    StudentID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    Email VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE,
    Age INT CHECK (Age >= 18 AND Age <= 100),
    Gender CHAR(1) CHECK (Gender IN ('M', 'F', 'O')),
    JoinDate DATE DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE,
    Status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'Active'
);

-- Course Table
CREATE TABLE Course (
    CourseID VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY,
    CourseName VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    Credits INT CHECK (Credits BETWEEN 1 AND 6),
    DeptID INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (DeptID) REFERENCES Department(DeptID)
        ON DELETE SET NULL ON UPDATE CASCADE
);

-- Enrollment Table (Links Student and Course)
CREATE TABLE Enrollment (
    EnrollmentID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    StudentID INT NOT NULL,
    CourseID VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL,
    EnrollmentDate DATE DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE,
    Grade CHAR(1) CHECK (Grade IN ('A','B','C','D','E','F')),
    FOREIGN KEY (StudentID) REFERENCES Student(StudentID)
        ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (CourseID) REFERENCES Course(CourseID)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    UNIQUE (StudentID, CourseID)  -- Composite unique key
);
examples.explanation: This comprehensive example shows: PRIMARY KEY (entity integrity), NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK (domain integrity), DEFAULT values, FOREIGN KEY with various ON DELETE/UPDATE actions, and composite UNIQUE constraint to prevent duplicate enrollments.

examples.title: Python SQLAlchemy - Defining Integrity Constraints
examples.description: Demonstrates using SQLAlchemy ORM to define tables with integrity constraints in Python
examples.code:
from sqlalchemy import Column, Integer, String, Date, ForeignKey, CheckConstraint, UniqueConstraint
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship

Base = declarative_base()

class Department(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'department'
    dept_id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    dept_name = Column(String(50), nullable=False)
    
class Student(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'student'
    student_id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = Column(String(50), nullable=False)
    email = Column(String(50), unique=True)  # UNIQUE constraint
    age = Column(Integer)
    
    __table_args__ = (
        CheckConstraint('age >= 18', name='age_check'),  # CHECK constraint
    )

class Enrollment(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'enrollment'
    enrollment_id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    student_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey(
        'student.student_id', 
        ondelete='CASCADE',  # ON DELETE CASCADE
        onupdate='CASCADE'   # ON UPDATE CASCADE
    ), nullable=False)
    course_id = Column(String(10), ForeignKey(
        'course.course_id',
        ondelete='RESTRICT'
    ), nullable=False)
    
    __table_args__ = (
        UniqueConstraint('student_id', 'course_id', name='unique_enrollment'),
    )
examples.explanation: This Python code shows how to define integrity constraints using SQLAlchemy ORM: CheckConstraint for domain validation, unique=True for UNIQUE, ForeignKey with ondelete/onupdate for referential actions, and UniqueConstraint for composite keys.

examples.title: C Program - Simulating Constraint Checking
examples.description: Pseudocode in C style showing how constraint violations might be checked programmatically
examples.explanation: This C-style pseudocode demonstrates how database constraints would be implemented at application level: entity integrity (primary key validation), domain integrity (NOT NULL, CHECK), and referential integrity (foreign key validation) checks before INSERT operations.

examples.title: Altering and Dropping Constraints
examples.description: SQL commands to modify constraints on existing tables
examples.explanation: This shows how to manage constraints on existing tables: ADD constraints, DROP constraints, disable/enable constraints for bulk operations, and query constraint information from system tables.
